elements.sent-2411-001: Large systems are often coarse-grained in order to study their low-dimensional macroscopic dynamics, yet microscopic complexity can in principle disrupt these predictions in many ways.
elements.sent-2411-002: We first consider one form of fine-grained complexity, heterogeneity in the time scales of microscopic dynamics, and show by an algebraic approach that it can stabilize macroscopic degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-003: We then show that this time scale heterogeneity can arise from other forms of complexity, in particular disordered interactions between microscopic variables, and that it can drive the system's coarse-grained dynamics to transition from nonequilibrium attractors to fixed points.
elements.sent-2411-004: These mechanisms are demonstrated in a model of many-species ecosystems, where we find a quasi-decoupling between the low- and high-dimensional facets of the dynamics, interacting only through a key feature of ecological models, the fact that species’ dynamical time scales are controlled by their abundances.
elements.sent-2411-005: We conclude that fine-grained disorder may enable a macroscopic equilibrium description of many-species ecosystems.
elements.sent-2411-006: Complex systems, including ecosystems, tend to be studied either through the prism of a few macroscopic observables (e.g. material stocks and energy fluxes~\cite{chapin2002principles ), or as interaction networks of many microscopic entities (e.g. species or individuals).}
elements.sent-2411-007: These two approaches rarely interface, since they differ in many of their goals and tools, such as describing specific low-dimensional attractors with nonlinear dynamical models, or generic high-dimensional phenomena with disordered models.
elements.sent-2411-008: Key results from the latter approach, such as complexity-driven instability~\cite{Bunin2017,hu2022emergent , focus on micro-level dynamics in systems that are trivial in the absence of disorder.}
elements.sent-2411-009: We investigate here the macroscopic consequences of adding fine-grained complexity to a system with nontrivial (out-of-equilibrium) coarse-grained dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this letter, we start from a small set of macroscopic observables $f_\alpha$, $\alpha=1\dots n$, each defined by a coarse-graining $f_\alpha(\Vec x)$ over many underlying microscopic variables $x_i$, $i=1\dots S\gg n$, whose dynamics follow \beq \dfrac{dx_i {dt =r_i G_i(x_i,\Vec f).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We will study the effects of adding disorder either in $\Vec r$, or as fine-grained interactions i.e. when $G_i$ depends directly on the other microscopic variables $x_j$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our motivating example here is a class of models of ecological community dynamics where microscopic variables, now representing biological populations or species, undergo multiplicative growth: \beq \dfrac{dx_i {dt = r_i x_i g_i(\vec x) \eeq where $g_i$ models species interactions, whether direct or mediated by the macroscopic variables $\Vec f$ (e.g. taxonomic groups or ecological functions).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: A key feature of this class of systems is that species with smaller abundances tend to exhibit slower dynamics; in particular, at a positive equilibrium $\vec x^\star>0$, as $g_i(\vec x^\star)=0$, the Jacobian matrix takes the form $J_{ij =r_i x_i^\star \partial g_i / \partial x_j$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Thus, if fine-grained interactions create heterogeneity in equilibrium abundances, they also induce a spread in time scales.
elements.sent-2411-015: We propose that introducing heterogeneity in the microscopic time scales, either directly or indirectly through interactions, can stabilize the dynamics of the coarse-grained variables $\Vec f $, near and even far from equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-016: We first analyze this mechanism around a fixed point in the general case of \eqref{eq:microdyn where heterogeneity is introduced directly into the rates $r_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: For ecological models following \eqref{eq:dyna_eq , we then demonstrate that macroscopic stabilization occurs when adding quenched disordered interactions between species, even though this addition also has a destabilizing effect on the microscopic dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-018: We illustrate these two distinct effects of disordered interactions in simulations, then show analytically via Dynamical Mean Field Theory that they are independent, and that the stabilization effect is indeed only due to the impact of interactions on microscopic time scales.
elements.sent-2411-019: Fixed point stabilization by time scale heterogeneity: Let us first consider the general model of \eqref{eq:microdyn , where disorder acts on the $r_i$ only, and study its Jacobian matrix around a given fixed point \beq J_{ij =r_i \left(\delta_{ij \dfrac{\partial G_i {\partial x_i + \sum_{\alpha ^n \dfrac{\partial G_i {\partial f_\alpha}\dfrac{\partial f_\alpha {\partial x_j \right) \eeq Assuming stabilizing microscopic self-interactions, i.e. ${\partial G_i /{\partial x_i < 0$, we can rescale $r_i$ and rewrite the above in the form \beq J_{ij = - r_i \left( \delta_{ij - \dfrac{1 {S \mu_{ij \right) \eeq where $r_i > 0$ and $ \mu$ is a rank $n\ll S$ matrix with $O(1)$ coefficients that captures the macroscopic dynamical modes.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The matrix $J$ has a bulk of negative eigenvalues stemming from the diagonal component, and a few outliers, associated with the eigenvalues of $\mu$, that determine whether the system is linearly stable.
elements.sent-2411-021: The question is thus: how does the spread in the row coefficients $r_i$ of ${J}$ impact its outlier eigenvalues? To gain intuitions, let us consider the $r_i$ and increase their spread continuously, while following outlier eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-022: Starting from equal rates $r_i$, we let them follow a random walk in a coordinate $\tau$ according to $r_i(\tau) \rightarrow r_i(\tau) + \delta r_i(\tau)$, where $\delta r_i(\tau)$ is taken to be a Gaussian variable of intensity $\delta \sigma(\tau) = \sqrt{r_i(\tau) \rd \tau}$.
elements.sent-2411-023: The factor $\sqrt{r_i(\tau)}$ ensures that rates do not become negative but similar results are obtained without this scaling.
elements.sent-2411-024: We use Woodbury's formula (see Appendix A) to derive an implicit expression for the eigenvalues of ${J}$, and then differentiate it to obtain their evolution equation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Under simple conditions detailed below, we find that each outlier $\lambda$ changes according to \begin{equation}\frac{\rd \lambda}{\rd \tau} = - \lambda \left(\int \frac{\rd r \rho_\tau(r) r}{(\lambda + r)^3}\right)\left(\int \frac{\rd r \rho_\tau(r) r}{(\lambda + r)^2}\right)^{-1} \label{eq:eigval_evol}\end{equation} where $\rho_t(r)$ is the density function of rates at $\tau$.

elements.sent-2411-079: Conclusions: In a system with out-of-equilibrium dynamics originating at the macroscopic scale, the addition of microscopic complexity can have a stabilizing effect -- in particular when it creates a spread in the characteristic time scales of microscopic trajectories, as in \eqref{eq:pseudo_jacobian .}
elements.sent-2411-080: This spread can be a direct consequence of heterogeneous rate parameters, or, as we found, an indirect consequence of disorder in interactions (nonlinearity is then required, as this effect arises in how the Jacobian matrix differs from the bare interaction matrix).
elements.sent-2411-081: This stabilization mechanism qualitatively recalls a phenomenon found in systems of coupled oscillators, where a diversity in intrinsic time scales can induce the emergence of a stable equilibrium~\cite{de2003coherent,kaluza2010role,saxena2012amplitude,zou2018amplitude .}
elements.sent-2411-082: In both cases, a mismatch of intrinsic timescales between coupled variables seems to be key in damping the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-083: Yet, our work puts more emphasis on the tension between levels of description: here, an instability is induced by macro-scale interactions, and damped by micro-scale disorder.
elements.sent-2411-084: This holds even when the micro-scale consequences of adding disorder tend to be a loss of stability (i.e. the well-studied destabilizing effect of complexity~\cite{May1972 and transition to high-dimensional chaos~\cite{Opper1989,Bunin2017,arnoulx2024many ).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The ecological systems studied here could provide archetypal examples of this phenomenon (as perhaps other systems with multiplicative growth properties, e.g. in economics~\cite{gabaix1999zipf ).}
elements.sent-2411-086: Recently, high-throughput methods for acquiring data on ecological and especially microbial communities have revealed ubiquitous fluctuations and heterogeneity at species and sub-species levels~\cite{grilli2020macroecological,goyal2022interactions .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Yet, more macroscopic descriptions, based on large taxonomic groups or ecological functions, are markedly stable across systems and over time~\cite{Louca2016,Goldford2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: It remains to be seen whether this macroscopic stability has other causes (e.g. physico-chemical constraints or simple aggregation effects) or truly results from species-level disorder damping ecosystem-level dynamics, and why this stabilizing mechanism might not already act at species level from sub-species disorder.
elements.sent-2411-089: While our mathematical analysis only captures local transitions to and from a fixed point, the qualitative intuition of the stabilizing mechanism seems to hold more generally: for instance, the 4-group system in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 switches from chaos to cycles before the fixed point is stabilized.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Understanding these transitions analytically provides a theoretical challenge that we believe is worth addressing in the future.

elements.sent-2411-249: In conclusion, using low-dimensional models to describe the effects of non-linearities on macroscopic observables is common practice in ecology and other complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, this approach ignores the effect of fine-grained heterogeneity on the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-251: In this Letter, we have embedded the macroscopic description in a more detailed microscopic one.
elements.sent-2411-252: This creates an interplay between the two levels of description that can only sustain macroscopic oscillations if the microscopic-variables remain sufficiently coherent.
elements.sent-2411-253: Increasing disorder thus leads to a series of low-codimensional bifurcations that tend to suppress macroscopic fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-254: The change of fixed point stability is the simplest example of such bifurcations.
elements.sent-2411-255: We have shown that quenched disorder results in a mismatch in the intrinsic timescales of the microscopic degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-256: As their dynamics become less coherent, the amplitude of macroscopic fluctuations decreases, in a phenomenon similar to the amplitude death phenomenon observed in strongly coupled oscillators\cite{de2003coherent,kaluza2010role,saxena2012amplitude,zou2018amplitude,demonte_2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-257: While we have only characterized the location of such transition for group-symmetric systems, numerical simulations show that stabilization occurs also in more general situations, and that stabilization can indeed involve transitions between out-of equilibrium regimes reminiscent of the bifurcations cascades observed for low-dimensional dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-258: The exploration of such transitions is an interesting future avenue of investigation.
elements.sent-2411-259: Similarly, future work should address how the SO/AF transition stems from an interference between the macroscopic dynamics and the unstable fixed points explored in~\cite{ros, arnoulx2024many .}
elements.sent-2411-260: Finally, we expect our results to be relevant to other fields where multiplicative growth is expected, such as economics~\cite{gabaix1999zipf .}
elements.sent-2411-261: Our study raises a fundamental question: what is the scale of aggregation most relevant for addressing stability? Indeed, disorder has long been known to have a destabilizing effect on microscopic dynamics~\cite{May1972 through the FP/AF transition.}
elements.sent-2411-262: However, this transition is asynchronous and does not generate macroscopic fluctuations beyond finite-size effects.
elements.sent-2411-263: Thus, only synchronous transitions are relevant for macroscopic fluctuations, and we have shown that, for these, disorder can have a stabilizing effect.
elements.sent-2411-264: This is particularly relevant in ecology.
elements.sent-2411-265: When the functioning and resilience of an ecological community depends on the stability of a few macroscopic observables such as the abundance of functional groups \cite{tikhonov2021 , our model suggests that fine-grained heterogeneity could make communities more robust.}
elements.sent-2411-266: On the contrary, we expect ecosystems whose function depends on a small set of species (so-called 'keystone' \cite{Doak ) to be fragile to heterogeneity-induced extinctions.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Recently, high-throughput methods for acquiring data on ecological and especially microbial communities have revealed ubiquitous fluctuations and heterogeneity at species and sub-species levels~\cite{grilli2020macroecological,martin-platero,rogers2023,Gore2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-268: Yet, more macroscopic descriptions, based on large taxonomic groups or ecological functions, are markedly stable across systems and over time~\cite{Louca2016,Goldford2018,fuhrman,mutshinda .}
elements.sent-2411-269: It remains to be seen whether this macroscopic stability has other causes (e.g. physico-chemical constraints or simple aggregation effects) or truly results from species-level disorder damping ecosystem-level dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our results demonstrate that, in order to understand the dynamics of a complex system, it is useful to discern its behaviour at different levels of description simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-271: If this may be difficult in many practical situations, microbial communities -- where the dynamics can be assessed at the level of functional groups, but also down to the strain level~\cite{goyal2022interactions, desai -- offer a particularly attractive opportunity for determining what kind of heterogeneity-induced transitions are most relevant for real systems.}
